segments.title: The Altmark incident and neutrality under pressure
segments.summary: The Altmark incident shows why neutral Norway could not satisfy British demands to free prisoners and German demands to preserve the ship's passage at the same time.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the February 1940 Altmark incident as the campaign's political starting point. HMS Cossack pursued the German support ship into Norwegian waters to free prisoners captured by Admiral Graf Spee, while Norwegian authorities had already allowed Altmark to travel south. The Norwegian officers faced an impossible-looking choice: a search that supported Britain risked German retaliation, while refusing one made Norway appear to favour Germany. The episode matters less here as a boarding story than as a compact demonstration of the strategic pressure on a neutral coastal state.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts Altmark, Cossack's attempted search, and Norway's conflict between British and German demands.

segments.title: Why neutrality needed a credible defence
segments.summary: The introduction argues that a neutral state can pursue peace, but it must also make clear that violating its sovereignty will be costly.
segments.body: The video connects Norway's predicament to the Labour government's approach to foreign and defence policy after 1935. Dr. Clarke contrasts a declared commitment to peace with the practical need for preparation, invoking Switzerland, Sweden, and Finland as examples of neutral states that invested in national security. He also frames the Anglo-German Naval Agreement as an effort to set observable limits on German rearmament rather than a simple endorsement of it. The central lesson is strategic: neutrality is more durable when backed by forces and institutions capable of defending it.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke discusses Norwegian government choices, German rearmament, and the relationship between neutrality and defensive strength.

segments.title: Mobilization politics and Norwegian readiness
segments.summary: Norway's leaders chose a gradual, discreet reserve mobilization, a decision that left a dispersed force poorly prepared for a sudden attack.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke outlines the political and military leadership decisions behind Norway's limited readiness in 1940. He focuses on the difference between general mobilization and the partial, quietly announced call-up that the cabinet selected for a reserve-heavy army. That approach could work against a threat appearing at one point, but it assumed time and a manageable axis of attack. The video stresses that fear of provoking war and uncertainty about whether Britain or Germany posed the immediate danger shaped this cautious posture.

evidence.note: The transcript covers the defence minister, cabinet choices, and the partial mobilization of Norway's reserve forces.

segments.title: Operation Weserübung attacks several ports at once
segments.summary: Germany's simultaneous thrusts toward Narvik, Trondheim, Bergen, Kristiansand, and Oslo defeated the assumptions behind Norway's gradual mobilization plan.
segments.body: This watch point maps the opening logic of Operation Weserübung. Dr. Clarke contrasts Norway's theoretical manpower with the smaller German first wave, then explains why the comparison misleads: German forces struck multiple strategically important places at once. He identifies the naval groupings and troop contingents directed against Narvik, Trondheim, Bergen, Kristiansand, and Oslo, while noting Norway's northern forces were oriented toward the Soviet frontier. The operational problem was not simply numbers, but surprise, geographic dispersion, and the inability to bring reserves into action quickly enough.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the German first wave, multiple landing groups, Norwegian force distribution, and the effect on mobilization.

segments.title: Denmark's invasion and the Norway campaign timetable
segments.summary: The April 1940 attack gave Norway very little time: Germany's advance into Denmark and Norway was coordinated with forces already moving toward Norwegian objectives.
segments.body: The final section returns to the consequence of delayed preparation once Denmark was invaded on 9 April. Norway began mobilizing, but did so partially and discreetly in the hope of gaining time without appearing to panic or provoke either belligerent. Dr. Clarke argues that Denmark was not merely a preliminary step before the Norway operation; it also helped Germany bring later echelons and supplies north while the opening forces were already under way. He closes by flagging later episodes at Narvik and the losses inflicted on German forces, rather than presenting the campaign as effortless.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Denmark's invasion, Norway's partial mobilization, German reinforcement, and the Narvik phase to follow.
